item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have this terrible habit of promising to rewrite my subpar works and never getting to it. But in a more ideal world where I had my shit together, that would be my response in order to placate and address your critiques. I'm sorry that deep thinking made you dislike this piece, that this piece is not up to the standard of quality I try to uphold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't really like this a lot. It's just nice, with italics and all. The dreamlike evocative explanation dilutes the feels you try to convey. The "Ancient pre-modern civilization destroyed by spookies" trope has been beaten to death, revived and beaten some more. The interview feels like a crutch, and a rickety one at that. Nevermind that; it's worse than a crutch, it's a narrative wheelchair, a rickety wheelchair, mind you. The cross-link, I don't care about, and you shouldn't either if you want a skip to stand on its own. Cross-links should be the icing of the cake, not the fudge you use to keep the levels together. For my part, I never check the cross-links other than to see if I have already read them.

Other than that, the skip is very well done. I actually upvoted before commenting, but after reading my critique I decided to change my upvote to a downvote (I don't believe in no-voting).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not that anyone cares but I feel like this is my most underrated SCP.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I feel like this crosslink doesn't add to the narrative of the SCP. I dig what you're going for, but that'd be better if this were Prometheus Labs tech, or if Prometheus Labs had gotten ahold of the tech that's involved here. But from reading that story, they haven't. I'll do some digging and edit this post or whatever with SCPs that I think would be more suitable for crosslinking.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: edits vetoed by author for the time being

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This skip's subject is brain-computer interfaces, which is a common concept on this wiki. I feel like the GOI Format GRANT REQUEST FOR INVESTIGATING THE APPLICATION OF CERTAIN RESEARCH ASSETS IN OVERCOMING INHERENT LIMITATIONS OF THE HUMAN BODY is the best way to link the concepts of this skip and human-brain interfaces together, but I could also be convinced to link SCP-191 or SCP-2048, though I don't think they apply to this skip as much as the GoI format mentioned above.

Editing this:

SCP-2473-1 refers to eleven identical chairs designed as brain-computer interfaces.

To this:

SCP-2473-1 refers to eleven identical chairs designed as brain-computer interfaces.

item.title: Re: Just a thought...
item.content:encoded: The big thing is, from a plot perspective, there's no real difference between this as a publicly-known artifact and a Foundation-contained one if the only resolution is, "Well, we know this is a thing now." I feel that archaeologists knowing fully where it comes from adds more to the story.

item.title: Re: Just a thought...
item.content:encoded:
an archaeological artifact studied by mainstream science with a near-total understanding of its origins.

Or with little to zero understanding of what happened. I mean, archaeological finds tend not to be very clear on the exact circumstances of their findings (at least, at first).

item.title: Re: Just a thought...
item.content:encoded:
1. SCP is purely speculative. Why can't our fiction take place in the future?
  2. This already takes place in the future. Not breaking new ground by pushing it further along the timeline.

That demographic isn't one I'm worried about.

item.title: Re: Just a thought...
item.content:encoded: Obviously the "SCP is contemporary, period" crowd would object. I don't know if they are significant enough to worry about.

item.title: Just a thought...
item.content:encoded:
What if I rewrote this as "Explained?" Instead of taking place in the near-ish future, I'd write it as I headcanon it in the distant future: an archaeological artifact studied by mainstream science with a near-total understanding of its origins.

What do y'all think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I didn't mention your music idea because I knew you'd wanna do it :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this. The imagery in the interview was interesting and brought quite a bit of backstory to the table. I also enjoyed the Foundation researcher being disgruntled that after pulling the device apart, putting it back together, they still have the anomalous origins label on there.

+1. For what its worth, it is a fun article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A more-positive reaction is what I was going for with the edits. I know I can't win all battles, but knowing my writing has improved is a victory in itself. I made edits regarding your feedback, but don't know how to edit regarding your last point of feedback. Maybe settle for two interviews, the first being the VR/dream-sequence, and the second being the more blatant storytelling? One of the central tenets of this piece is that the entity inhabiting the ship is still, on a certain level, human, but it's forgotten how to be human, how to behave around other humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Commentary
– hide block

It may sound weird, but I really like that first footnote.

All hardware within SCP-2473 appears to have suffered several centuries of oxidization.

This is out of place; it should go before the sentence mentioning the recycling system failure in the previous paragraph.

I still like the second footnote, and continue to think it's a good example of how the new crosslinking should be used.

You use 'discreet' twice in the final paragraph of the description. Maybe switch up the modifier for the military base.

Researcher Begay: So, what did you do? What happened next?

No need to reiterate the researcher's title here.

Researcher Begay: So what happened next?

Dr. Moodley: Take your time, there's no rush. We want you to remember as much as possible.

This strikes me oddly, only because I expect Moodley would be talking to Begay, not the D-Class.

[sic]]

The interview very definitely feels spliced together from the original three. Might not seem that way to someone who hadn't read the previous version, but I dunno. It's certainly long. Overall, I have a more positive reaction to it, but it's still a dream skip.

item.title: Let's try this again, shall we?
item.content:encoded: This is a soft rewrite, not a total rewrite. Mostly killing clunk. I couldn't think of a good way to do the complete rewrite…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'm at a no-vote, for reasons others have discussed above, but I sort of feel like I should specify that the story just wasn't very interesting.

+ show block
– hide block

They're pre-modern humans, but you never went into any implications about that. It decided to land at an obscure military base, a point also unexplored. The story told by the end was: a little girl from another planet that was facing an unspecified bad thing accidentally got launched alone in a spaceship, and then eventually died but the spaceship uploaded her.

(And the damn no-face thing. I'm not entirely sure what you intended, but this wiki has made me endlessly tired of the "AND THEY DIDN'T HAVE <facial feature/s>!!" as a stylistic choice.)

I don't feel like I missed anything you mentioned in the discussion thread or missed and subtle story cues, the story just never did anything to really pay of the original mystery of "Woo, ancient alien spaceship!" in a satisfying way.

I didn't watch the short video above, but this really feels like a description of a music video for a certain kind of rock band, with a self-contained narrative that either ties up at the end of the video or serves as a half-cliffhanger for their next single, not particularly directly tied to the lyrics. The kind that are mildly pleasant to watch, but they're not Contact/The Arrival/pick your think-y sci-fi feature. You've successfully evoked that mental imagery, but the story never developed past that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are things I like and things I don't like about this piece, which balance themselves out to a no-vote.

+ Long-windedness incoming
– Long-windedness incoming

There are aspects of the writing which, to be brutally honest, I find annoying.

First, (as Vezaz pointed out) endless repetition of "apparent", "apparently", "appears to", and "seemingly", which is boring and dilutes the punch of whatever you're writing. If you want to have a versimilitud-istic "hey we're scientists so we leave room for alternative explanations!", you can get that out of the way in a single sentence: you could preface the interpretation of the craft's systems and function with a sentence saying "we've made the following conclusions based on our study of the spacecraft, but we're not 100% sure", or bundle that admission into a footnote near the beginning. And then you can cull every "appear" synonym thereafter.

Second, you've overused the "quotation marks as a way of indicating uncertainty or describing something by analogy" device. ('Payload', "V", "bottom" of the "V".) It smacks of lazy writing — i.e. there exists a more accurate, precise way of conveying one's meaning, but it's hard to compose, so the writer's resorted to a hand-wavy, milquetoast description. I'm not entirely innocent of this sin, so apologies for the hypocrisy in casting the first stone, but it's avoidable 99% of the time. The quotes on 'payload' aren't necessary — and if you really want to put across the uncertainty of rigorous scientists there, you could go for [obvious/clear/definite] payload. With "V", just V-shaped would have been fine, and instead of "bottom" you could have said [apex/point/vertex/tip]. You could also have described the shape as a chevron or arrowhead.

Third, there's a just a little too much technobabble. Not an egregious amount, but enough to choke up the narrative space. e.g. is it absolutely necessary for your story to say "gravity and visual sensors have been identified"? And that the solar panel is "hexagonal in shape and approximately 8 meters in diameter"? Adding scientific details to a scip is like adding spices when cooking — you need a pinch to bring the piece to life, but too much can be overpowering.

Fourth, the interview logs read like prose rather than natural speech. You hit some of the classic natural speech indicators — hesitancy, repetition, filler words — but the sentence structure is way too perfect throughout. People stop-and-start sentences a lot when talking irl, go back on themselves to add affirmative interjections ("so it was like, yeah, like I was saying before"), use sentence fragments, miss out pronouns, that kind of thing. Also, given that these interview sequences are dream-like, I'd expect her to describe them like how actual folks describe their dreams: progressive aspect ("I was walking" rather than "I walked"), re-doing their descriptions because they didn't quite get it right the first time, lots of demonstrative determiners ("there were these big clouds"), focusing on really specific, slightly absurd aspects ("the trees had these little pink flowers shaped like aeroplanes") etc. There's also some unsubtle exposition, especially that vague, slightly implausible "I just had this narratively useful sense of nostalgia, you know?"

But what I did like is the story itself. I felt genuine emotion bubble up inside of me. Scips don't often make me feel sentimental, so well done. In terms of development and pay-off, you delivered the core of the narrative pretty well. The comment from Wolfrover, and Sergeimosin's bit about treating the D-class like a human being, give a good explanation as to how that was achieved.

So — I can't believe I'm ending yet another crit with this conclusion, insufferable misery guts I am — genuine emotional punch, but let down by some aspects of the writing. No-vote.

item.title: At Least One Success . . .
item.content:encoded:
I can honestly say that I "got" the basic message, and that's without reference to the linked SCP or the videos. Of course, I'm the kind of guy who cried watching Titanic (and not just because they messed up some of the history), so take that for what it's worth. :)

On the whole, very evocative. Of course there's no image of the girl's face; "she" doesn't remember it anymore. And the entire piece works very well on a scientific level, too.

Upvote from me, and I don't give them lightly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think that the Foundation is tentative about drawing conclusions about anomalies. However, I think, in this case, it's pretty damn obvious what each thing is, so I have made the edits you suggest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Appears" to be a re-entry vehicle, appears to have an ion drive, appears to have a cockpit - all those "appears" stick out like sore thumbs, be specific.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible by popular request
item.content:encoded:
The narrative from a d-class alone?

I think it would serve to streamline the story and let it move quicker, though if you wanted to present a warning message with this article using that cross link, I'd say it would hurt it. It depends on how you'd revise the story's presentation, which as I said, just feels lacking in depth.

I'm not sure how exactly you could go about rewriting this, since many people already seemed to enjoy it. Taking another step away from Shelter wouldn't hurt, though, since the whole "girl creating worlds in her space pod" aspect was interesting in itself and I would want this thing to take a similarly dark path.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible by popular request
item.content:encoded: One's opinion on a piece isn't gonna be cause for hostility on my part. Do you suppose the idea I sent Scantron's way is a good place to take this?

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible by popular request
item.content:encoded:
I realize critiquing this may have messed up the presentation for me. Still, I guess it really is just opinion when I say that the narrative just feels long-winded and lacking in points of interest among the way, for a story that could have been communicated in just one interview log.

Sorry bout that. No hard feelings, hopefully.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me, a traditional narrative from the D-Class and/or researcher perspective might be more effective at sending the message home. Do you think I could pull that off better than this?

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible by popular request
item.content:encoded: The thing is, my hope for this article was that readers wouldn't take it at full face-value, that it'd get them thinking on bigger implications and allow them to draw their own conclusions. I was trying to make a point of letting the reader take the information presented (non-anomalous but anachronistic spacecraft, unreliable narrator hiding in the spaceship, a story of a planetary exodus as told by the unreliable narrator) and think about the bigger picture (pre-modern human civilization, a broken witness to that civilization and its fall). I don't know how I could better present that without spelling it out; I was intentionally oblique. Iunno, man.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible by popular request
item.content:encoded:
I already understood all of this, except:

The people who built this were scared. The ship left early, with only one passenger aboard. There were supposed to be dozens more ships, each with 11+ crew. Whatever scared them got them first.

I've thought on two more problems for me as well:

  • First off, referring to the quoted passage above, I didn't really receive any indication of urgency from the adults other than them being busy and yelling with each other once. The fact that the girl goes to space and then goes back in a very small part of the article framed this story to seem more like an isolated accident, with a narrow perspective of the actual story (running whatever's in space).
  • along with what Communism said, the story meandering and such, there really isn't anything to it besides the story: no world-building, no interesting history from this society, because it all comes from an unreliable narrator who by all appearances is just an average young girl. There was nothing to indicate that the culture and society the girl grew up in was any different from present day, as she did "normal little kid stuff".

I know you said you wanted to do something "scientific", but the rigid nature of this anomaly hurts it more than helps it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what, if anything, you could add or subtract from this piece as it stands that would solve the basic problems I had with it. My suggestion is to figure out what story in particular you're trying to tell, then find another vehicle to tell it — perhaps not a literal vehicle this time, and maybe something that doesn't involve extended metaphorical sequences and meandering into technical details. The details are up to you, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeh, that'd work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Howzabout "bandages were applied to minimize blood loss," then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
bandages were applied at puncture points to minimize injury.

I think you mean 'infection', since she's already been injured.

I'll say this, since I've been very against the crosslinking: connecting two skips via reference to a third, completely unknown skip is a pretty darned clever idea. That there's more to it that just that is also good.

If anything kept me from upvoting, it's probably the lengthy interviews that often feel like a narrative more than someone talking. Also the fact that it's basically a dream skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see where you're coming from. There's supposed to be a lot of dependence on understatement, fridge logic, but if I didn't pull through, that's on me. Care to make any suggestions on improving the piece?

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: They're similar stories but I made a point of being as different as possible because plagiarism is bad.

item.title: Spoilers in collapsible by popular request
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block
  • There's a communication gap between the D-Class and what's left of the pilot. There's unreliable narration going on.
  • The girl in the sim is a digital copy of the pilot. She's not all there anymore.
  • The people who built this were scared. The ship left early, with only one passenger aboard. There were supposed to be dozens more ships, each with 11+ crew. Whatever scared them got them first.
  • The autopilot wasn't sure what to do, so it hid and tried to keep the little girl's mind safe until it thought the monsters were gone and the girl could finally get to her original destination safely.

I hope that helps. Lemme know if there's a way to make that clearer.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Damn. I've seen the video, I can't believe I missed the reference

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Going down your list, gonna say a little 'bout every paragraph.

  1. Yeah, D-Class abuse doesn't make much sense I think.
  2. Sitting in a computer for 20,000 years does funny things to a girl. Does a number on her people skills, if nothing else.
  3. The Foundation needs governments to get along with its operations. Sometimes they gotta give world governments a few inches so they won't try to take a mile.
  4. You got catheters running out of a mummy's orifices and into a weird machine, it's either life support or a sex thing. Foundation assumed life support because that's slightly less horrifying.
  5. The radiocarbon dating wasn't a total bust, and there were other ways to determine dates.

Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This spent a LOT of time communicating something that made me feel very little. The monologue itself wasn't really enjoyable in its own right — I had to tolerate it to get to the real meat of the information.

Overall, the SCP seems to mostly be a vehicle for a meandering story with an unspectacular narrator — it's a format I've had my fill of, and I didn't really enjoy myself at any point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the hell, If you had told me you were inspired by THAT video, I might have been able to offer some more substantial advice. :P

Even with the connection to 1795 that you've pushed and knowing what you're going for, I feel like you haven't communicated what you've imagined to someone that doesn't know what's going on. Hell, I'm not sure myself how this is supposed to make me feel, other than sad for the girl. I thought there'd be a bit more dread?

If you could explain what's going on here to me, that may help. All I'm feeling is confused by the different details I know about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… Thoroughly impressed with quite a few things here. I like seeing the Foundation interacting with D-Class in a way that doesn't involve simply stuffing them into an SCP and then shooting them when the experiment is over. The fact that a doctor was assigned to evaluate the D- and her injuries in this one really helped my suspension of disbelief.

Also extremely pleased with the fact that the entity was reduced to using memetic visual metaphors to convey their message, as opposed to the old Scifi trope of "Well they read your mind and learned English" or "English with gibberish thrown in, because… look shiny spaceship!".

I also love that the Foundation was forced by the nature of the object and its trajectory to actually acknowledge its existence to the world governments and negotiate for the right to keep this item.

I have only two nitpicks:

"The apparatus for recycling human waste failed before recovery"

How does the Foundation know the apparatus is for recycling human waste if it's failed? It's exo-technology, so it could be for literally anything, and without observing its function, this feels like guesswork.

"the current working estimate is of 20,000 years (±5,000 years) between death and recovery"

I'd like some elaboration on how they got to this number. As is, and with the stated fact that carbon dating is inconclusive, it feels entirely arbitrary to simply stick that number there.

Other than that, I think this brings quite a bit to the table. Well done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Glitch Mob is great, but you forgot my additional theme music proposal... which you knew I was gonna link at least five more times between you pitching the draft and you posting this

Seriously though, this is probably my favorite thing you've written. I love the space drama aesthetic, and this also has some elements of SOMA that are perfect.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Inspiration.

Theme music.

Thanks goes to Cyantreuse, Apoplexic, Jack Ike, Aiden Eldritch, Cimmerian, and whoever the fuck else looked at this in chat.

collapsed for spoilers
– hide block

The cross-link has greater significance than it initially appears to have. That's all I'll say.

I hope you all enjoy!
